sourceDesc.p: Downloaded from https://medium.com/@louBurnard/yes-it-can-be-done-ad5839dabc15, published on Medium 2015-02-01 and translated from HTML to TEI.

body.head: Yes it CAN be done
body.p: So there I was listening to the radio news on Saturday (17 January 2015), when they announced that the channel tunnel had just been shut down on account of a flaming lorry (I use the words advisedly: last time this happened, the continent was cut off for weeks). Which was bad news for those, like me, planning to travel to Tours via a tightish connexion in Paris the following day. I spent an indecent amount of time investigating economically viable alternatives (bus to Heathrow, flight to Paris, total cost one way in excess of £300, no I think not) and somewhat to my surprise came up with the following. And here’s how it went.

It’s the one you can just see approaching over this chap’s shoulder. It’s free. It comes every five minutes or so, though less often on Sundays. It has wifi which occasionally works, and which on this occasion enables me to confirm that Eurostar services are running, but with delays of “up to an hour”. A delay of 20 minutes would be enough for me to miss my connexion at Montparnasse.
body.quote: 10:35 Start walk across town from St Giles to the station…
body.p: There is a bus reminding me that one day not so far away I will be able to get to Marylebone via the Chiltern Line, but not today. Indeed, any thoughts of a last

minute change of plan in favour of the Eurostar route are firmly put aside when I enter the strangely silent station building. Yes, it’s Sunday, maintenance work is in progress, and there are no trains to London anyway. So I settle down to wait for a train to Birmingham Airport instead.
(from platform 1, since it’s the only one working today)
body.quote: 12:45 Catch silly monorail across to the real airport
12:55 Consider, briefly, the excessive amount of silly signage
body.p: I always arrive too early at airports. Last time I went through security at BHI they found (but graciously permitted me to post home) an offensive opinel. This time I forgot to extract my Samsung tablet from the backpack, and also to put my toothpaste into a plastic bag. All of which meant that I just arrived at the departure gate in time, somewhat breathless, as they were starting to board. Airports, bah.

figure.head: All aboard, and waiting for blast off

body.quote: 13:42 Flight BE 3007 Non-stop Airbus A318 service to Paris is now fully boarded and ready to depart
body.p: (And despite code-sharing with AF1065, MU 8602, and indeed MK9337, the flight was pleasingly empty) I concentrated on getting my slides ready, as per usual, and almost failed to catch the moment when suddenly the clocks went forward an hour, and the plane started its descent on to the solid ground of la belle France.
body.quote: 15:57 (French time) the long process of landing begins
body.p: And then of course, once they have let us out, we can start the endless journey up and down and round about the twisty maze of corridors and zones and terminals that is Charles de Gaulle Airport
body.quote: 16:08 Yes, it’s an airport: keep walking
body.p: You are in Terminal 2 zone D, area K, and that is not where you want to be. Keep walking.
body.quote: 16:16 Oh how nice it’s a shuttle.
body.p: Let’s see where this one takes us…
body.quote: 16:29 You are in Terminal 2, area B.
body.p: Clever. Keep walking. The railway station is nearby, allegedly.
body.quote: 16:35 Just in time for the non-stop service to Gare du Nord!
body.p: (Which is a distinct improvement on the trains that stop everywhere) Mind you, it’s still the RER B, so it then goes on to stop everywhere subsequently. Which is fine, since we are going to change trains at Saint Michel.
The RER is fast but subterranean and post-apocalyptic. The metro has more charm, let’s face it, even la ligne 4 which snakes across the map like a lost soul.

figure.head: 17:31 Dont forget to allow for that damnable tunnel between Montparnasse-Bienvenue (the metro) and the Gare Montparnasse proper.

body.quote: 17:35 Up the escalators to the real Gare de Montparnasse (1 & 2)
body.p: It’s still Sunday, but I have never seen this station empty. I am feeling smug as it looks as if I am going to catch my 18:16 departure to Tours without even breaking into a sweat.

figure.head: And here she is. TGV 8327, all the way to Tours in first class confort.

body.p: where I sit down and complete work on my talk for tomorrow. Well, nearly. The gig is a re-run of one I gave a year ago, so it’s only a matter of reconciling myself to not having improved on it much. And into Tours station we roll, on time at 1930, giving me plenty of time to check in at my favourite Hotel, wash my face, and get to La Chope in time for an excellent dinner with Marie-Luce.

figure.head: Wild salmon and chicory. Much nicer than it looks in this rubbish photo

body.p: Conclusions? Total time door to door: 8 hours. Had I caught the Eurostar, it would have been almost exactly the same. The cost was also much the same. All the same, I took the Eurostar home — and even though they were still making dire warnings about delays of up to an hour going through the tunnel (a week later!) in the event it arrived back at St Pancras only 15 minutes late.
